name: Kasakrtsna
dates_notes: Pre-Bādarāyaṇa; cited in Brahma-Sūtra 1.4.22; no work survives.
dating_methodology: Same evidential structure as Auḍulomi and Āśmarathya: terminus ante quem fixed by Bādarāyaṇa's citation in the *Brahma-Sūtra* (1.4.22), terminus post quem fixed by the developed Upaniṣadic problematic he addresses. The conventional placement of the *Brahma-Sūtra* circa 200–100 BCE (Nakamura, *A History of Early Vedānta Philosophy*, 1949, vol. I; Deussen, *The System of the Vedānta*, 1883) sets the upper bound. Belvalkar (*Shree Gopal Basu Mallik Lectures on Vedānta Philosophy*, 1929) and Sharma (*The Brahma-Sūtra*, 1965) treat Kāśakṛtsna as the latest of the three voices in the 1.4.20–22 cluster on internal grounds: his position resolves the dialectic the first two open. Pāṇini's *Aṣṭādhyāyī* mentions a *kāśakṛtsni* (4.1.114; cf. 6.1.154) as a grammatical-school name, and Patañjali's *Mahābhāṣya* refers to a *Kāśakṛtsna-Mīmāṃsā* — possibly the same lineage, possibly distinct. If the identification is accepted (Hiriyanna, *Outlines of Indian Philosophy*, 1932, treats it as plausible), Kāśakṛtsna stands in a circle of pre-Pāṇinian or contemporary-with-Pāṇini teachers, which would push his upper bound back toward the fifth or fourth century BCE. Note (AI-generated audit): the Pāṇini identification is not philologically settled; a conservative dating brackets him between fourth and second centuries BCE, which is the window adopted here. Modern scholarship (Nakamura) treats his position as the dialectically strongest of the three and reads Bādarāyaṇa's silence as endorsement, but this reading is itself a Śaṅkaran inheritance.
core_thesis:
Kāśakṛtsna's position is preserved in two words at Brahma-Sūtra 1.4.22: *avasthiter iti kāśakṛtsnaḥ* — 'because of [Brahman's] standing-in [the *jīva*], says Kāśakṛtsna.' The single technical term *avasthiti* (locative-stative, from *ava-sthā*, 'to abide, to stand within') carries the entire doctrinal claim. The grammar is genitive-object: the supreme Brahman *abides in* the *jīva* — not as a part of the *jīva*, not as a manifestation that contrasts with an unmanifest source, but as the standing-condition of the *jīva*'s being. The contrast with the two preceding voices is exact. Āśmarathya at 1.4.20 says the *jīva* is non-different by *abhivyakti* (manifestation, with the asymmetry of source and manifest); Auḍulomi at 1.4.21 says non-difference is the truth of the liberated state. Kāśakṛtsna at 1.4.22 says Brahman *stands within* the *jīva* always, in both bound and liberated phases, with the apparent distinctness an artefact of conditioning rather than a feature of Brahman's relation to the self.

The doctrinal force of *avasthiti* is contested in a way the citation cannot resolve. Śaṅkara's *Bhāṣya* on 1.4.22 reads *avasthiti* as full-strength identity: Brahman abides in the *jīva* in the way that one and the same thing abides as itself, the apparent distinctness being only *upādhi*-conditioned superimposition. On this reading, Kāśakṛtsna is the proto-Advaitin within the *Brahma-Sūtra* itself, and Bādarāyaṇa's silence after 1.4.22 — no further sūtra adjudicates — counts as endorsement. Modern scholarship qualifies this. Nakamura (vol. I, 1949) and Sharma (1965) note that *avasthiti* is grammatically ambiguous between strict identity (Brahman *is* the *jīva*'s being) and inherent presence (Brahman is *located in* the *jīva* without being numerically the same). The first reading underwrites Advaita; the second reading is compatible with a Viśiṣṭādvaita-style inner-controllership doctrine, where Brahman as *antaryāmin* abides in but is not identical with the *jīva*. The historical Kāśakṛtsna cannot be made to disambiguate; what he held with certainty is the standing-condition relation, and the dispute over its modal force is post-citation.

What the position commits to, on the weaker reading, is already philosophically substantial. Brahman's *avasthiti* in the *jīva* makes the *jīva*'s consciousness, agency, and very being constitutively Brahman-grounded; it is not that the *jīva* is one thing and Brahman another that happens to oversee it, but that the *jīva*'s standing-as-self is the standing-as-self of Brahman within it. This is incompatible with any *bhedābheda* in which *bheda* and *abheda* are equally fundamental, since *avasthiti* is asymmetric: Brahman abides in the *jīva*, not vice versa. It is also incompatible with the strong dualism Madhva will later develop, since *avasthiti* is not the relation of one independent substance to another. The position occupies, on the citation-evidence, a coherent middle: weaker than Śaṅkara's strict identity, stronger than any *bhedābheda* that treats *bheda* as ontologically primary, and recognizable as the structural ancestor of the inner-self / inner-controller doctrines that both Advaita and Viśiṣṭādvaita will systematize.

What the citation does not preserve is Kāśakṛtsna's argument. We have *avasthiteḥ* — the abstract noun in genitive — as the reason; we do not have the dialectical demonstration. The reading that treats Bādarāyaṇa's silence as endorsement (Śaṅkara) and the reading that treats it as merely the closing of the *adhikaraṇa* (Bhāskara) cannot be settled by the text. What the text settles is narrower: Kāśakṛtsna held the *avasthiti* thesis, and the thesis is structurally the strongest position in the cluster.

engaged_works.title: Positions reconstructed from Brahma-Sūtra citations
engaged_works.ascription_notes: No work survives; the proto-identity-thesis is reconstructed solely from Brahma-Sūtra 1.4.22.
engaged_works.summary: A single sūtra preserves Kāśakṛtsna: Brahma-Sūtra 1.4.22 — *avasthiter iti kāśakṛtsnaḥ* — the third position in the cluster on the *jīva*'s relation to Brahman that begins with Āśmarathya's *abhivyakti*-thesis (1.4.20) and Auḍulomi's *mokṣa*-state non-difference (1.4.21). The technical term *avasthiti* (genitive *avasthiteḥ*, 'because of [Brahman's] abiding within') is the entire doctrinal content: Brahman stands within the *jīva* as the standing-condition of the *jīva*'s being. Śaṅkara reads this as strict identity: Brahman *qua* the *jīva*'s essential being is numerically one, the distinctness being *upādhi*-superimposed. Bhāskara and the *bhedābheda* commentators read it as identity-with-real-distinction: Brahman abides in the *jīva* without ceasing to be the cosmic source. Rāmānuja and Viśiṣṭādvaita read *avasthiti* as the *antaryāmin* relation: Brahman as inner controller abides in the *jīva* without numerical identity. The grammatical form does not settle the dispute; the abstract noun in the genitive of cause supplies the *because*-clause for Kāśakṛtsna's view but not the metaphysical specification of *how* Brahman abides. Modern philology (Nakamura, *A History of Early Vedānta Philosophy*, vol. I, 1949) reads the position as the dialectically resolutive third voice in the *adhikaraṇa* and treats Bādarāyaṇa's silence as either endorsement (Śaṅkara) or as conventional closure of the *adhikaraṇa* (Bhāskara). Beyond this single locus and its commentarial afterlife, nothing of Kāśakṛtsna survives. The *Mahābhāṣya*'s mention of a *Kāśakṛtsna-Mīmāṃsā* is suggestive but cannot be verified as the same author.

quoted_by.what_quoted: Śaṅkara's strict-identity reading of *avasthiti* as the resolutive voice of the cluster.
